question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: What the records explicitly document: A nearly identical incident was logged as KF11: Management Report not working (TI-722511). The problem occurred reproducibly in all environments, affected different users and machines, and showed slow Report Designer performance and intermittent errors. The documented diagnostics and remediation steps performed were: Data Mart Reset and recreation of the FRDB in the integration environment; opening a Microsoft support ticket (#2404050050000982); and later applying IISCrypto best-practice cipher suites and restarting AOS servers. The ticket shows the Data Mart reset and FRDB recreation were performed, the Microsoft ticket was opened, and ultimately IISCrypto changes and AOS restarts were applied

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: What the records explicitly document: A matching incident (KF22 / TI-1548823) showed Outlook clients could receive but not send; OWA and mobile worked. Root cause there was SMTP AUTH being disabled by tenant Security Defaults and affected clients were legacy Outlook builds. The documented remediation was selectively re‑enabling SMTP AUTH for affected users and adjusting Conditional Access policies; after that sending worked and mailflow was monitored

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: What the records explicitly document: This is the same symptom as KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds (TI-1604879). The documented root cause there was a Session-Expires / NAT session timeout mismatch: a new Voice VLAN had UDP NAT session timeout set to 30s while other sites used 180s. The documented remediation was to adjust the NAT/session timeout per the Direct Routing architecture (SBC session parameters and NAT timeout), after which calls remained stable
context:
=== CITED SOURCES ===
SOURCE 16:
﻿
Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds
Ticket number: TI-1604879
Company: Customer Company 52 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 16
Owner: Employee 35
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 22.07.25 09:14
Closure
Closure date: 31.07.25 15:52
Closure text: Analysis completed. Adjustment made according to Direct Routing architecture. Calls stable.
Activities
22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk
Good morning
Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds.
The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time.
Internal Teams calls are not affected.
Could you please review this?
Kind regards
22.07.25 09:14
Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35.
22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry
Good day
Thank you for your report.
Could you please confirm:
– Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls?
– Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration?
– Are all locations affected or only a specific one?
Thank you very much.
22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response
It only affects the Basel location.
Both incoming and outgoing calls.
The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier.
Home office users are not affected.
22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note
Test performed with affected user.
External call to mobile phone.
Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds.
No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.”
23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis
Possible client error or unstable connection.
Test with another device → same behavior.
Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change.
23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer
Good day
We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs.
Signaling is established correctly.
The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client.
Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location?
Kind regards
23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response
On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented.
A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created.
Could this be relevant?
24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis
SBC logs reviewed:
– SIP INVITE successful
– 200 OK correct
– Call established
– After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC
No obvious error code.
24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis
No blocking visible.
Ports open according to standard configuration.
24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis
Session-Expires header reviewed.
Session timer set to 30 seconds.
Comparison with other locations shows:
There, 180 seconds.
25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation
Document:
“Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1”
Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant.
Note in the blueprint:
For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination.
25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests
Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC.
Test call performed.
Call remains stable for several minutes.
Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible.
28.07.25 – Coordination with network team
New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy.
UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds.
Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds.
29.07.25 – Adjustment
NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.
30.07.25 – Customer feedback
Good morning
We conducted several test calls.
No more disconnections observed.
Longer conversations are also stable.
Thank you very much.
31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 16
The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure.
The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture.
Calls now remain stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: What that implies for your case (documented actions you can request): Check the voice VLAN/NAT policy UDP session timeout and align it to the standards used at other locations (increase from 30s to 180s or to the architecture value). The record documents changing NAT session timeout and SBC session parameters as the fix

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Basel location printers not responding and jobs disappearing were caused by a newly created printer VLAN where the print server could not reach printers (missing firewall/routing rule). Fix: adjust network/firewall rules so the print server can reach the printer VLAN (per Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard) [TI-1622483, KF60]
context:
=== CITED SOURCES ===
SOURCE 22:
﻿
Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF60: Location printer Basel not responding to print jobs
Ticket number: TI-1622483
Company: Customer Company 60 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 15
Owner: Employee 21
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 06.11.25 08:37
Closure
Closure date: 12.11.25 14:52
Closure text: Network and queue configuration adjusted according to Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. Function restored.
Activities
06.11.25 08:25 – Email from Contact Person 15
Good morning
Since yesterday, the printer “MFP-Basel-01” is no longer responding to print jobs. The documents remain in the queue, and in some cases disappear without being printed.
Other printers in the building are functioning normally.
Could you please check this?
Kind regards
06.11.25 08:37
Ticket created and assigned to Employee 21.
06.11.25 09:10 – Initial analysis
Remote access to an affected user.
Print job briefly appears in the print server queue, then disappears without printing.
Queue itself is not paused.
Print Spooler service is running.
Test print directly from the print server to the printer also fails.
06.11.25 10:05 – Inquiry to customer
Good day
Were there any recent changes to the network or infrastructure at the Basel location?
Thank you for your brief feedback.
06.11.25 11:20 – Customer response
Last week, a new network segment for devices was introduced. The printers were moved into a separate VLAN.
No other changes are known to us.
07.11.25 09:00 – Further analysis
Ping from print server to printer unsuccessful.
Ping from a client within the same location network successful.
This indicates segmentation between the server network and the printer VLAN.
Comparison with document “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”.
In the section Location Concept, it is defined that printers operate in a separate VLAN but must be reachable from the print server. Routing and firewall rules must be configured accordingly.
07.11.25 11:30 – Internal coordination with Network Team
Confirmation that the new firewall rule did not allow the server network access to the printer VLAN.
Rule adjusted according to standard.
10.11.25 – Test phase
Printing from print server successful.
Multiple test prints from user workstations performed.
No abnormalities observed.
12.11.25 14:40 – Customer feedback
Printer is functioning normally again.
Larger print jobs are also processed correctly.
12.11.25 14:52 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 15
The root cause was a missing network allowance between the print server and the newly configured printer VLAN at the Basel location.
The configuration was adjusted according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”. The printer is fully reachable again.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?

Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF60: Incorrect printer automatically set as default
Ticket number: TI-1623017
Company: Customer Company 60 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 18
Owner: Employee 23
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 18.11.25 08:49
Closure
Closure date: 24.11.25 15:11
Closure text: Printer assignment and default configuration corrected according to Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. Behavior stable.
Activities
18.11.25 08:35 – Email from Contact Person 18
Good morning
Several employees in the Finance department report that their default printer automatically switches to “MFP-Zuerich-01”, although they work at the Basel location.
They must manually select the correct printer before each print job.
The issue seems to have started last week.
Kind regards
18.11.25 08:49
Ticket created and assigned to Employee 23.
18.11.25 09:20 – Initial analysis
Remote connection established with an affected user.
Default printer is indeed “MFP-Zuerich-01”.
Both Basel and Zurich printers are visible in the printer list.
Comparison with a user from another department shows the correct location printer set.
18.11.25 10:05 – Inquiry to customer
Good day
Were there any recent organizational changes or group adjustments in the Finance department?
Kind regards
18.11.25 10:40 – Customer response
Yes, the Finance team was moved into a new AD group last week.
The old group was deactivated.
19.11.25 09:10 – GPO assignment analysis
Comparison with “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”.
In the section Printer Provisioning it is defined that:
• Printer assignments are location-based via AD groups
• Default printers per location are set via GPO
• No parallel groups with identical function may exist
Review shows that the new Finance group is additionally a member of a higher-level Zurich location group.
As a result, the Zurich printer is also mapped via GPO and set as default.
19.11.25 11:30 – Test
Temporary removal of the incorrect group membership.
gpupdate /force executed.
User logged in again.
Default printer now correctly remains “MFP-Basel-01”.
20.11.25 – Coordination with customer
Confirmation that the group structure had not been fully cleaned up during the reorganization.
Correction performed according to blueprint specification.
24.11.25 14:50 – Customer feedback
Multiple users tested.
Default printer remains correct.
No further automatic switching.
24.11.25 15:11 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 18
The root cause was an inconsistent group membership following an organizational adjustment.
Printer assignment was corrected according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”.
The correct location printer now remains permanently set as default.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

Test print directly from the print server to the printer also fails.
06.11.25 10:05 – Inquiry to customer
Good day
Were there any recent changes to the network or infrastructure at the Basel location?
Thank you for your brief feedback.
06.11.25 11:20 – Customer response
Last week, a new network segment for devices was introduced. The printers were moved into a separate VLAN.
No other changes are known to us.
07.11.25 09:00 – Further analysis
Ping from print server to printer unsuccessful.
Ping from a client within the same location network successful.
This indicates segmentation between the server network and the printer VLAN.
Comparison with document “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”.
In the section Location Concept, it is defined that printers operate in a separate VLAN but must be reachable from the print server. Routing and firewall rules must be configured accordingly.
07.11.25 11:30 – Internal coordination with Network Team
Confirmation that the new firewall rule did not allow the server network access to the printer VLAN.
Rule adjusted according to standard.
10.11.25 – Test phase
Printing from print server successful.
Multiple test prints from user workstations performed.
No abnormalities observed.
12.11.25 14:40 – Customer feedback
Printer is functioning normally again.
Larger print jobs are also processed correctly.
12.11.25 14:52 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 15
The root cause was a missing network allowance between the print server and the newly configured printer VLAN at the Basel location.
The configuration was adjusted according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”. The printer is fully reachable again.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds
Ticket number: TI-1604879
Company: Customer Company 52 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 16
Owner: Employee 35
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 22.07.25 09:14
Closure
Closure date: 31.07.25 15:52
Closure text: Analysis completed. Adjustment made according to Direct Routing architecture. Calls stable.
Activities
22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk
Good morning
Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds.
The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time.
Internal Teams calls are not affected.
Could you please review this?
Kind regards
22.07.25 09:14
Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35.
22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry
Good day
Thank you for your report.
Could you please confirm:
– Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls?
– Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration?
– Are all locations affected or only a specific one?
Thank you very much.
22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response
It only affects the Basel location.
Both incoming and outgoing calls.
The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier.
Home office users are not affected.
22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note
Test performed with affected user.
External call to mobile phone.
Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds.
No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.”
23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis
Possible client error or unstable connection.
Test with another device → same behavior.
Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change.
23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer
Good day
We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs.
Signaling is established correctly.
The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client.
Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location?
Kind regards
23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response
On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented.
A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created.
Could this be relevant?
24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis
SBC logs reviewed:
– SIP INVITE successful
– 200 OK correct
– Call established
– After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC
No obvious error code.
24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis
No blocking visible.
Ports open according to standard configuration.
24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis
Session-Expires header reviewed.
Session timer set to 30 seconds.
Comparison with other locations shows:
There, 180 seconds.
25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation
Document:
“Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1”
Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant.
Note in the blueprint:
For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination.
25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests
Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC.
Test call performed.
Call remains stable for several minutes.
Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible.
28.07.25 – Coordination with network team
New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy.
UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds.
Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds.
29.07.25 – Adjustment
NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.
30.07.25 – Customer feedback
Good morning
We conducted several test calls.
No more disconnections observed.
Longer conversations are also stable.
Thank you very much.
31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 16
The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure.
The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture.
Calls now remain stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds
Ticket number: TI-1604879
Company: Customer Company 52 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 16
Owner: Employee 35
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 22.07.25 09:14

Closure date: 31.07.25 15:52
Closure text: Analysis completed. Adjustment made according to Direct Routing architecture. Calls stable.
Activities
22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk
Good morning
Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds.
The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time.
Internal Teams calls are not affected.
Could you please review this?
Kind regards
22.07.25 09:14
Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35.
22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry
Good day
Thank you for your report.
Could you please confirm:
– Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls?
– Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration?
– Are all locations affected or only a specific one?
Thank you very much.
22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response
It only affects the Basel location.
Both incoming and outgoing calls.
The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier.
Home office users are not affected.
22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note
Test performed with affected user.
External call to mobile phone.
Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds.
No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.”
23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis
Possible client error or unstable connection.
Test with another device → same behavior.
Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change.
23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer
Good day
We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs.
Signaling is established correctly.
The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client.
Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location?
Kind regards
23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response
On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented.
A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created.
Could this be relevant?
24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis
SBC logs reviewed:
– SIP INVITE successful
– 200 OK correct
– Call established
– After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC
No obvious error code.
24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis
No blocking visible.
Ports open according to standard configuration.
24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis
Session-Expires header reviewed.
Session timer set to 30 seconds.
Comparison with other locations shows:
There, 180 seconds.
25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation
Document:
“Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1”
Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant.
Note in the blueprint:
For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination.
25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests
Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC.
Test call performed.
Call remains stable for several minutes.
Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible.
28.07.25 – Coordination with network team
New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy.
UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds.
Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds.
29.07.25 – Adjustment
NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.
30.07.25 – Customer feedback
Good morning
We conducted several test calls.
No more disconnections observed.
Longer conversations are also stable.
Thank you very much.
31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 16
The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure.
The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture.
Calls now remain stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

Comparison with “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”.
In the section Printer Provisioning it is defined that:
